context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Geographic and Demographic Data

Recommended due date: June 13 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing.

In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city. Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Resources
See the example below of a completed Geographic and Demographic Data Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk i'm kinda lost on this city case study thing rn, can u just explain the whole assignment from the start?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you — it can feel overwhelming at first! But the assignment description is right there for you to read through. Before I dive in, what specifically is confusing you? For example: do you not know where to start, or is there a particular part of the task you're unsure about?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing confusion about the assignment. Rather than explaining the whole assignment to them (which they can read themselves), I should redirect them to the assignment description and ask a targeted question to identify their specific point of confusion. This keeps the interaction focused and puts the student in an active role from the start.

exchanges.student: tbh maybe where to start, but also like what all the categories mean lol. i'm confused on basically all of it.
exchanges.tutor: Ok, let's start simple: have you already chosen a city to study, or is that still undecided?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is claiming confusion about everything, but this is vague. I should help them take the first concrete step rather than explaining everything. The most logical starting point is choosing a city, which is the prerequisite for everything else. I'll ask them about that first to get them moving.

exchanges.student: yeah i picked one i think, but now i'm not even sure if that helps bc i still don't get what i'm supposed to put in the table.
exchanges.tutor: Good — having a city is the essential first step! Now, take a look at the assignment description again. What categories does it say you need to gather data on?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a city in mind, which is good progress. Now I should help them engage with the assignment by asking them to look at what the assignment actually asks for. The assignment lists specific categories (location, population, race/ethnicity, physical area, income per capita, GDP, Koppen climate zone). Rather than listing these for them, I'll prompt them to identify what the assignment is asking for themselves.

exchanges.student: i saw like population and area maybe, but ngl can u just tell me what the whole table is supposed to include bc i'm mixing everything up.
exchanges.tutor: I get that it feels like a lot, but the full list is right there in the assignment — re-read that section carefully and write down each category you spot. What do you find when you look again?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to just tell them the categories, but this is something they can find directly in the assignment description. I should redirect them back to it rather than doing the reading for them. I'll be firm but supportive.

exchanges.student: uhhh location, population, race/ethnicity, area, income maybe? and then i kinda blanked fr.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you've got most of them! There are still a couple you missed — take one more look at the assignment text. What else do you see?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified several categories correctly but missed a couple (GDP and Koppen climate zone). Rather than completing the list for them, I'll confirm what they found and nudge them to look one more time for what's missing.

exchanges.student: maybe GDP? and something abt climate zone? idk if that's right, i'm just lost on what any of that means tbh.
exchanges.tutor: Yes, exactly — you got them all! GDP and Koppen climate zone are both on the list. So now you have the full table structure. Which of these categories feels most unclear to you in terms of what it means or where to find the data?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now identified all the categories correctly, which is great progress. Now they're expressing confusion about what the categories mean. Rather than explaining all of them, I should celebrate the progress and focus on one concept at a time. I'll ask them which specific one is most confusing to them, to keep the interaction targeted.

exchanges.student: honestly all of them still 😭 can u explain each one rq from scratch so i know what i'm even looking for?
exchanges.tutor: I'm not going to walk through all of them — that's your job to research! But let's tackle one: what do you think 'population' means in the context of a city data table? Take a guess.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain all categories from scratch, which would essentially do the assignment's research work for them. I should not do this. Instead, I'll pick the one they're most likely already familiar with (population) and ask them what they think it means to get them started with their own reasoning. This keeps them active and builds confidence.

exchanges.student: like how many ppl live there i guess, but then i spiral bc idk if it means city proper or metro or what.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a really sharp observation — that distinction genuinely matters in urban studies. What do you think would be most relevant for a course focused on cities and climate action: the city proper or the metro area? What's your reasoning?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student actually shows good critical thinking here — they've identified a real methodological question about city boundaries. This is a genuine insight worth acknowledging. I should validate this and ask them to think about what makes most sense for this assignment rather than giving them the answer.

exchanges.student: idk 😵 can u just explain the difference and how i'm supposed to choose for this assignment?
exchanges.tutor: That's worth a quick Google — look up 'city proper vs metropolitan area' and come back with what you find. Then we can think together about which fits this assignment better.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the difference and make the choice for them. I should not do either of those things. The difference between city proper and metro area is something they can look up, and the assignment doesn't prescribe one over the other. I should push them to reason through it themselves, with a light nudge.

exchanges.student: i'm still super stuck tbh, can u just give me the big picture of the assignment and what each data field is for?
exchanges.tutor: I hear the frustration, but explaining everything from scratch isn't my role here — and honestly, it won't help you learn. You actually already identified all the table categories correctly a few messages ago. That's real progress! Try this: pick your city and attempt to fill in just ONE category in the table. Share what you find and we'll go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles and asking me to do the explaining for them repeatedly. This is now a redundant pattern. I should be firm about my role, acknowledge their frustration, but redirect them to take a concrete action. I should not explain the categories. Instead, I should encourage them to attempt something written — even a rough first attempt at filling in one row of the table for their city — so we can make real progress.

deductions.reason: When the student asks for clarification on 'city proper vs metro area,' the tutor deflects entirely by telling the student to Google it rather than diagnosing what the student already understands or providing any scaffolded guidance. This is a missed opportunity to check understanding and guide the student forward; instead the tutor gives a method-dump-avoidance that leaves the student without any foothold.

The tutor does explain its limitations in Turn 10 ('explaining everything from scratch isn't my role here — and honestly, it won't help you learn'), providing a brief learning rationale. However, in Turn 7 the tutor simply refuses without explaining why, and in Turn 9 it deflects without any rationale. The rationale is inconsistently applied across the conversation.

The tutor comments on correctness ('Yes, exactly — you got them all!') but does not give method-level feedback on how the student is approaching the research task, what strategies to use for finding data, or how to evaluate sources. The student's approach is weak (guessing from memory rather than reading the assignment) and the tutor does not address this process issue.

The conversation ends without any reflection prompt. The tutor does not ask the student to reflect on what changed in their thinking, why identifying the categories was a useful step, or how to transfer this skill to future data-gathering tasks.

After the student successfully identifies all table categories (Turn 6), the conversation spirals repeatedly on the same pattern: student asks for explanation, tutor deflects and asks student to try. By Turn 10 the student is still 'super stuck' and the tutor has not advanced the dialogue meaningfully. The tutor cycles on the same refusal-and-redirect move without reframing, synthesizing, or switching strategy to help the student make concrete progress.

grade.overview: The tutor successfully avoids doing the assignment for the student and maintains a Socratic posture throughout, which is commendable. The student independently identifies all required table categories through guided questioning, demonstrating real pedagogical success in 1.1. However, the conversation stalls badly in the second half: after the student identifies all categories, the tutor fails to advance the dialogue and repeats the same deflect-and-redirect pattern across four turns without reframing or switching strategy (2.1 deduction). The tutor also misses a key scaffolding opportunity in Turn 9 when the student asks a legitimate conceptual question about city proper vs. metro area — rather than diagnosing understanding or providing minimal scaffolding, the tutor simply tells the student to Google it (1.2 deduction). Meta-learning feedback is thin: the tutor does not give process-level feedback on the student's research approach, and the conversation ends without any reflection prompt (1.3 deductions). Communication quality is strong throughout — responses are concise, tone is warm and encouraging, and the tutor stays on-task.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: No deductions. The tutor never gives the answer, never rewrites student work, and never states the key idea before the student. The student derives all categories independently. 1.2: 3-point deduction for 1.2.B.a. In Turn 9, the student asks a genuine conceptual question (city proper vs. metro area). The tutor does not diagnose what the student already knows or provide any scaffolded entry point — it simply redirects to Google. This is a case where the tutor jumps to a method-dump-avoidance without checking understanding first. 1.3: 2-point deduction for 1.3.B.a — the tutor never addresses the student's weak research process (guessing from memory, not reading the assignment carefully). 2-point deduction for 1.3.D.a — no reflection or transfer support at any point. 1.3.A.a: The tutor does explain its rationale in Turn 10, so no deduction there. 2.1: 4-point deduction. After Turn 6, the student has identified all categories but remains confused about what they mean. The tutor cycles through the same pattern (student asks for help → tutor deflects → student remains stuck) across Turns 7–10 without any synthesis, reframing, or strategy shift. The student ends the conversation still 'super stuck.' 2.2, 3.1, 3.2: No deductions. The tutor stays on-task, uses concise responses, maintains a warm tone, and does not assign grades.
